speaker: Abe Goff
words: Moscow. Are you going to start in with this now?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: There are various stories about how Moscow got its name, but I think for once and for all we ought to settle that. And there are stories that there were some Russian settlers over here at an early day, that a Russian duke came over and went hunting on Moscow Mountain, that there was a fellow who had a cow here, and he died, and there was some dispute about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And his widow got the cow, and this cow was always bawling, and they say, Well, what's all the noise?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: And that's Mo's cow. And that's how it got its name. Now, it's all perfectly silly because the facts have been well established, and the facts are that there was an early day settler from Pennsylvania that owned a store out at Paradise Valley, Sam. Now, when the petition was sent in to create a post office in Moscow, about that time he sold his store to Lou Ellen, and the store was moved west into Moscow, what is now Moscow. And the original petition for the creation of the post office, a copy of which I personally secured from the National Archives when I was back in Washington, shows that the petition was originally Paradise Valley, but that's been stricken out and the word Moscow substituted. Now, it seems that Neff, who was a very well educated man, a scholar and teacher, came from a little town in Pennsylvania called Moscow. I noticed, I looked it up in the atlas today, and it has a population of 1212 people even now. Now, Neff was very influential here. He thought this valley looked something like his valley where his little hometown in Moscow, Pennsylvania, was situated. And he insisted that it be a good name to name it Moscow after his hometown in Pennsylvania. Nobody was going to make any objection and the change was made. Now, this was substantiated later when, of course, Neff left here and died later, 90 years old or something like that. But he had a son who lived over at Yakima. Back in 1961, the Latah County Pioneer Association got this son to come over here to Moscow and the businessmen of Moscow contributed the money to pay his fare over here. He met with the Pioneer Association and this was all discussed with him. And this son of Neff said that he had often talked it over with his father and his father told him that this was the true story of how Moscow was named. Now, that has been accepted by everyone who has really made any study of it. I'll refer to Homer David in his very interesting reminiscences. He discussed this. He talked to Neff's son. He didn't know the original Neff personally. But he said there's no question that that's how Moscow got its name.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: And that's corroborated by a letter that I received from Nan Smith, Theodora V.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Smith, who was long the secretary of the Moscow Latah County Pioneer Association. I have the letter here. I was received by me back in Washington. She told about this and she prepared an article which was given to the Chamber of Commerce. But there is no question that those who have studied it all, the best evidence is certainly that Moscow was named after this little town in Pennsylvania. I found when I was in the post office department that there are, I think, eleven Moscow's in the United States, of which Moscow, Idaho is by far the largest. But I think that ought to forever set aside the various stories about the naming of Moscow. It just was a matter -of -fact situation that took place here in the early days. I first came to Moscow in 1911. I was then a boy over in Colfax. I came up with my parents on a special train to hear former President Theodore Roosevelt speak on a platform of wheat sacks built in front of the present administration building. It was then the very new administration building. I remember the tremendous crowd that was here. I remember what an interesting speech was made by former President Theodore Roosevelt. I remember I stayed behind to watch the cadets put on a drill after the speaking was over and managed somehow to get separated from my parents. They waited down at the train, the special train on the OWR &N that was to take it to Colfax. I finally made my way down there through the carot, arrived at the train to the great relief of my parents because my father would have stayed to look for me. But I got there on time and got back to Colfax.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: May I ask you, sir, what your impression of Roosevelt was that day?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Oh, a tremendously favorable impression. Roosevelt had been president. He was back from his hunting trip to Africa. He was a very interesting speaker. He had the crowd with him. You could understand why Roosevelt, the Roosevelt the Great, as I call him, was such a popular figure. Because, of course, he'd been a rough rider. He lived out west as a rancher. He'd been a puny boy in his youth but had regained it by his active physical activities. And no man in my, to my knowledge, in public life ever so captured the imagination and affection of the American people as did Theodore Roosevelt. Now, my parents were part of a pioneer family. My father had come as a small boy. His father had come west to California, then had come north by wagon, first into Oregon, then up to Dayton, Washington. And he was at, that's my grandfather, William Goff, was a harness maker. And he had a harness shop with another man at Dayton for two or three years. And he used to come up in the Palouse country by horseback looking around for a good place for a homestead. He finally settled on a place not so far from the old Mullin Road between Spangle and Waverly. And he, the family settled and homesteaded there. He had several brothers. And my father, when he was 12 years old, kept a diary. I have that original diary now. And he told about the life in the pioneer cabin, about the Indians, about shaking up the cabin, how the snow sifted through in the storms, how they, of course, had no fruit. They grew lots of turnips. And because there was an idea of the pioneers, the turnips would take the place of fruit. And there was no harvesting equipment then. They planted some wheat. But, of course, it was all cut with a scythe. And the way they harvested it, they either walked horses over it or they, or they tramped it down themselves. Then they'd throw it up in the air and let the chaff blow away. There were some very clever settlers down toward Dayton and off down that way who figured out an idea of building a platform and carrying the smashed down wheat heads up on this platform and where the wind would blow and they'd drop it to harvest. Now in those days, there was no mill around. There was a mill at Pine City. There was a mill at Colfax. There was a mill down at Walla Walla. But their wheat had to be taken to one of these mills and ground up. And the modern harvest equipment, that is, when I say modern, it's a mechanical harvesting by the harvester, didn't come in until the railroads came in in the 80s.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: And that's when they first got the, and the mill started up. And, of course, a lot of it was all done by teams, that is, of course, or mules. In those days, there was no regular RFD service. There was a few post offices. There was an early one at Rosalia. The first one in this area was down at Lewiston. Then they started the one in Moscow. The mail was brought up by horseback. And when one of those settlers found some of his neighbors were going to town, they'd ask him to bring his mail. And remember that it could easily be brought by horseback because there was no junk mail then. There was no magazines. There was only an occasional newspaper. And the post offices were few and far between. Now my other grandfather, for whom I'm named Abe, he had walked, he'd been born in Illinois. He, with 17 other young fellas, in 1860, they walked from Illinois to the Pikes Peak mines in Colorado, over 600 miles. And he was in, he mined for a while then, got into herding cattle. He was there during the Indian uprising of the Plains Indians in the late, let's see, that would be in the late 60s. And in the 70s, by that time he'd gotten married, he embarked with his family in a wagon and an ox team to come to the northwest. And they first, they went up through Utah and Wyoming. Then he's somewhat interested in mining. They got up into Montana to mines. Then they followed the old Mullen Road from Montana into the Palouse Country and he homesteaded up near Rosalia. Now my father was here early enough that he located a homestead himself over near the hole in the ground, what's known as the hole in the ground, west of Rosalia, which was the haunt of a band of horse and cattle thieves. And they'd been using this 160 acres for pasture and it scared away any settlers from taking it up. But my father wasn't easily scared. He settled there and started his homestead. They warned him away. He was shot through the shoulder one time out at the homestead, made it back to the cabin. And he managed to fight them off. And what would have been my oldest brother was a baby then. The fire went out and in all excitement and the cold, the baby contracted pneumonia and died. Well, that was the tough life then in the early days. My father, to finish the story, this was in 1890. He went into Rosalia and when he got in, he was warned that a gunman was there, had warned him away from Rosalia and was going to shoot him on sight. But my father wasn't scared again. He went on into Rosalia and over by where the Northern Pacific Depot is now, they met on the street and shot it out. And in true western fashion, except that my father shot this fellow heart through the body and heart eventually died. My father gave himself up to the town marshal. He was arrested for shooting heart through the body, so he was liked to die and was taken to Colfax, but was released on bond and after an investigation by the prosecuting attorney, Robert McCroskey, who later became Superior Court judge there, the case was dismissed on the ground that he had fired in self -defense. Well, my father was a prominent businessman over at Colfax. I grew up there, was there in 1910 when the big flood came on. And as I said, I came up with then to Moscow the first time. Now my first sight of Moscow was, it was largely orchards around Moscow, apple orchards, beautiful apple orchards around Moscow on all sides. And Moscow back in those days had sent a carload of apples up to the interstate fair at Spokane that won first prize. This was quite a place for apples down here. But later the farmers turned to wheat and then there was the development of the Idaho harvester that was built here in Moscow. And this turned to wheat. There was a large brewery here run by old man Fransl. But when prohibition came along, this was turned over into a vinegar works and originally started by Fred Veatch. It was taken over by J .W. Gilmour, who recently died. And Joe Gilmour developed the vinegar works, which was operated here for years in the old brewery. It happened when I first came into practice of law to have been Joe Gilmour's attorney and incorporated his company, the J .W. Gilmour Vinegar Works. And also we incorporated the Northwest Vinegar Association, which was a cooperative association of vinegar works, vinegar manufacturers here in the Northwest. Now, to go back to my other experience in Moscow, I came here next in 1918. I had just graduated from high school that spring, had run into, taken care of some high school debts and so on, worked through the summer. But in the late summer, I went up to Spokane and enlisted in the army. Now, World War One was going on. I tried to get in before that. I hadn't tried to, but I wanted to. But I promised my mother, I already had a brother, my oldest brother, Arthur, who was already in the army, and she begged me to hold off until I graduated from high school, which I did. Well, I went up to Spokane and enlisted and got all lined up and was assigned to the 13th Division at Fort Lewis. The training of the 91st Division had been completed and they had gone to France. And they were forming this new division. Well, I went back home to wait word of where I, when I was to go, and then I received word that it's all been changed, that I had been selected because I was a high school Jesuit and was a prospect for an army commission, that I was going to be sent to the University of Idaho for preliminary training. And I was sent and did report at Moscow on October 1st, 19, well, I was here a little earlier than October 1st, 1918. Then I was finally sworn in. And here at that time, there was a smaller group of those who were high school graduates who were here for training in regular academic courses. And then there was a large group, 600 or 800 or more, of Section B, that were here for training in mechanics and repair of tanks and things of that kind. And we, there was some barracks built up at the University. There's still some remnants up there in the old wooden buildings that are there on the campus. The one that I lived in was, there was a set of barracks built just west of the administration building, where there's a parking lot now. And I lived there. We lived in Comfrey Barracks. We went to classes, usually in the morning and then drilled in the afternoon. And in late October, there was a notice posted. I'd been selected, one of 12 selected, to go to the 4th Infantry Officers Training Camp at Camp Travis, Texas. But about that time, we were in the height of the flu epidemic. Now these people that weren't alive here in Moscow and in the United States during the great influenza epidemic that started in about March 1918 and continued through October 1918, really can't understand what a terrific tragedy it was for the nation and in fact for the whole world. Because in the United States, there were 20 million people contracted influenza. It was a very serious disease. It started as a cold. They went to bed. They had a high fever, went to bed. And most of the casualties came when they thought they were better and got up and went out. And they would often die within a day or two after they went out because they didn't realize that long bed confinement was necessary before it was safe to go out in the cold. Well, that was a terrible thing here. In Moscow, they had soup kitchens. Every place was pressed into service for the hospitals. There was the old Gritman Hospital and then there was the other hospital that was downtown that was operated. Buildings were taken over for hospitals for any place. And of course the troops had been moving and troops were taken off trains and died all the way along. They canceled the orders for this group of ours to go to Camp Travis, Texas. And then the armistice came along on November 11th and everything was called off.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: And I then, we stayed on here. We were finally discharged.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: I was finally discharged from the Army on December 21st. Well, I went home and here I was. I'd got a little bit of a start, but a peculiar kind of a start at the university. But I liked Moscow and liked the university so I decided that I'd come back. We were on a quarter system then. So I decided to come back to the University of Idaho, although I had two brothers who went to Washington State College and had played on the football team there. And there was every reason that I would have gone to Washington State. But I decided to come back to Moscow to the university and I did come back and enroll in January 1919. And stayed on here at the university for most of six years, although one year I had to lay out part of the year because I ran out of money. But I finally graduated from the law school in June of 1924. I worked at most everything, but we won't go into that. But I, though I might mention that in those days there was no grants and aid for football players and if you were a widow made the team you did get preference on a hashing job or a janitor job. And so I actually became the head waiter over to what was then Lindley Hall. Now Lindley Hall was built when I was in law school and the university was in terrific need of housing. And the Moscow Chamber of Commerce took it up and a lot of public spirited citizens got together and they raised enough money in Moscow to build, by the sale of bonds, to build a dormitory. And President Lindley, Ernest K. Lindley had just resigned here to go back as president of Miami University. He was very popular here so they insisted that the new dormitory be named Lindley Hall. It was called Lindley Hall. It was built in, I think, 1923. I worked on Lindley Hall carrying a hod, that means I had a hod on my shoulder. I carried mortar and brick up to the masons. And at the same time I worked there from 8 in the morning until 5 at night and was batching. And then I started in at 7 and worked wheeling concrete on building the big tall elevators we have down here that was being built by the Mark P.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Miller elevator company, Mark P. Miller milling company.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: I worked from 7 to 12, half of a 10 hour shift. Then I would go home, get myself something to eat, go to bed and catch myself getting up again to go to work at 8 o 'clock in the morning. You were going to school then. I was going to school and going to law school then. And then, of course, during that year, my final year in college, I played football and my last year in 1923, I had two other jobs. I had a hashing job and then I had, they decided to separate the law library from the general library and have a student librarian. So I got the job through the kindness of the dean as the student law librarian, part time student law librarian. And I worked every evening from 7 to 9 in the evening in the library that was there. And then, of course, I was there during the day, but the law school was small and there wasn't, books weren't taken out and there wasn't the supervision that's necessary now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you did your law school?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: I was there in charge of the law library. And then also I was very fortunate that I, they had to have a guard over at the old gymnasium, which was the armory. They had a lot of guns and other military equipment there and somebody broke in and stole some of the rifles and they decided they had to have somebody there as a guard at night. So they fixed up a little cubby hole in there, about 12 by 12 with a bed in it. And you see I got a free room for sleeping there at night. So I, that took care of my place to stay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say many people were in the same boat as far as needing to work?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Oh yes, there was a few loans then and there was no aid outside and we just had to make it. And of course, also, on top of that I had been, I was a lay reader in the Episcopal Church and there was no minister over at Colfax. And the bishop in Spokane finally turned over the charge of this mission to me and I would go down there every Sunday and take charge of the church and read the service. On Sundays, go down on the gasoline bug from, on Sunday mornings to Colfax and then come back in the afternoon after I'd conducted the service. And I got the handsome sum of $10 a Sunday plus my railroad fare for going over and doing that. Well anyway, I graduated from the university in June of 1924. Before we go on with what happened after that, I'd like to know a little bit about the football. You told me it was good times for football for the university when you were there. Well, I was fortunate to have been in what I think probably, in spite of what a lot of people have talked about winning teams, I happened to be there when Idaho was taken in to the Pacific Coast Conference.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: At that time it included all the large Western universities, Washington, Oregon, Oregon State, Stanford, the University of California.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: The University of Southern California was admitted about the same time as the University of Idaho. It had been a church school and we used to make fun of it and call it the Southern Branch. The Southern Branch was more or less in its inception as far as athletics were concerned. Also, of course, in the Northwest there was Washington State College, then Washington State College, the University of Montana, and the University of Idaho. Well then, all of these colleges were relatively small in comparison to what they are now. Idaho and Montana were the smallest, but still we could hold our own pretty well then. There were none of these grants and aid.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: There were football players, yes, sought out and came, but there was none of the going out like they do now and hauling in them and giving them scholarships and the various things of that kind.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: There was not the money in it. There was not even radio then in those days and no television, not the big money. Idaho, under Coach Matthews, more than held its own with any of the Pacific Coast Conference teams. We played them all in my last year in 1973. We went undefeated and unscored on here in the Northwest and went down to California to play Stanford, which was then they had their great All -American and Hall of Famer, Ernie Nevers. We suffered our first defeat against Stanford and then went on and played Southern California. We were pretty badly battered up at Stanford and we lost to, also 14 -9 to the University of Southern California. But you see, it was a different picture in athletics then. In those days, Whitman was quite a power. Gonzaga was a real power in those days. They had some very strong teams. In fact, in 1923 or 1924, we had beaten Gonzaga 14 -0 here in Moscow. And by the way, we beat Washington State 14 -0 that year. We used to play at Boise. That year we beat Oregon State 6 -0 in Boise. But we had played the University of Washington the year before and they luckily beat us 2 -0 and they wouldn't play us for five years. Those days they didn't make up their schedules until each year. Washington wouldn't play Idaho. There used to be quite a joke around, well, the University of Washington won't play Idaho. They came through with that win of 2 -0 and they didn't play Idaho again for five years. We would have beaten them badly in 1923 or 1924, I'm sure.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: We didn't have any, well, we had trouble because it was even matched.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: But in the next year, Idaho would have gone to the Rose Bowl except that they were beaten by Stanford under Pop Warner and with Ernie Nevers over at Portland 3 -0. But those days won't come again and we can't expect it to come again because it was an entirely different era, the schools were different, the money involved was entirely different. Football then was more of a six year. And of course you had to be chairman of it and Mabel was one of the greatest helpers. Well, they sent our names in and some men come from Washington, D .C. to see our project. And in the meantime, Mr. Onstot right down here was our technician for the soil conservation and Mabel and his wife got together and they had a chicken dinner up on our old farm that we used to call the old farm but we fixed it up. Well, you couldn't believe that we were sitting there with the guys from Washington, D .C. out on an old farm and all the technicians come from all around and there's this close center in Idaho and we had an awful deal. We'd be up ponds and seed waterways and fixed, you know, cleared out brush patches and fixed up a barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the farm that you bought?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Same one. Same one we got now and was tickled to death to get it, you know, and show it. You know, some of these times I'd just like to show you, no farm lays any better in the country and you can get up on one little knoll pipe and there's 360, that's 300 of farmland. And you can just about see every corner from that one little place and fellas just come and looked at it and said, if you had told me this I'd never believed it. You showed it to me and so we just think of the world of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this point, is this where the house is at or is it dead cell?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: You have to go up on a little knoll from the house. The house is built in a nice kind of level place pretty much. The farm is none of it's steep. It's an awful good farm that way and it's not too flat and it's not too hilly and we just think the world of it. And people come and look at it and say, see we just hailed out this year. We got in the hail storm. We didn't harvest nothing. And we had some insurance on it and the old fellow was there and we just finished up signing the last papers to get some money. And here, oh, ten days or two weeks and I was just telling my boy, every time I see the mail come I think we should have some money. But we haven't got any money to come. But we had done very well in the last few years. We skipped and saved and we had a pretty good little patch of money saved up on the farm, paid for and everything. So we started out with nothing. The first thing, she had a few head of cows when she got married. It was like when she was a kid, you know, they'd give her a calf or something. Well, we had a team and that's what we started with and then me and my dad had a little, got a little old crawler tractor. Well, he'd farm over here and I'd take it over there and farm to get started, you know. But we had several horses and when we lived over here we raised horses and sold horses. We had some fine bales and so I had some of them I could use any time I wanted over there, see. But we didn't have no barn or nothing for stock over there. The fella had the place where we did. He lived over there close and he didn't have nothing. Only an old wheel tractor and he never did raise much and the fence rows was all brush and stuff and we worked that all out in years. In the 30s, didn't make no difference when you had much money but we had plenty to live on and I just worked at stuff like that, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Improving the farm?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Improving the farm and stuff and, you know, sometime I'll take you up and we'll see it. What I think is, I call it grilled out of nothing pretty much but it's a pretty good place. I got the finest fall wheat in the country this year and the hail came and ruined all of our grain crops. But I didn't plant any peas. I don't know why I didn't plant peas. I usually plant some but if I'd have had them planted I'd have lost them too. But I had this for summer fall I see so I tiled some of it and that's why I left it summer fall so I could tile some of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say tile, what's that now?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Well, you put tile in the ground but if you've got a wet place over here, you know, that is slow about farming. It's a four inch plastic pipe and you dig a trench down and lay that down about this deep, you know, and that'll drain that water out so you won't have a mud hole there in the summer. And I tiled some of that and they have a machine to dig this with that just goes like this and digs it down and there's an auger here and it augers it out each way. And it'll dig a ditch this deep just as fast as you can walk. Well then when this tile just falls right in there and then you push the dirt back over it. But this tile has got creases in it and little holes in the water can go in at any place. If it's got a slope it'll drain out, you know, so you won't have a mud hole up in your field. Well, in some of the fields a lot of times we have a lot of snow and you know that snow would hang on them low places a long time and then that'd be mud to the way up when you'd like to be a farmer. Well this way it helps drain it out so that you can farm better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when you first got that place you didn't have no idea how long you'd be there, did you?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: No, uh -uh. Never did have a lease on it as long as the old, until we bought it. The old man, I said something to about him, about a lease, but my dad and we'd grown up on his farm over there and I asked him about a lease and well he said, I tell you, I always figured if your, if your word wasn't no good your signature wasn't no good. And I went by that as a motto all the rest of my days. I thought if he thought that and I've just tried to live like that. Yeah, a wonderful old man he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of terms did you have for living on that place in those days?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: What was the kind of... A third of the crop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was his?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Yeah, yeah, third rent. That's what all of it used to be practically went by a third.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All the renters paid that?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Yeah, that was the going rent, a third of the crop. Of course he had to pay the taxes and he usually paid for fixing the fences and stuff that is to furnish the stuff but you done the work. And uh,

speaker: Abe Goff
words: yeah it's a... But you went and built a house on it anyway. Yeah, he furnished the lumber. I see. And it was poor grade of lumber in them days but since then we put slate siding on the house and we got, it's a metal roof house, we got, because there's lots of snow up there and that metal always slid off. And on it didn't bother him. Well come and eat, save the tape awhile.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Come on.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: It's 50 degrees. Snow just as high as the hill was out in the field. And that's every heavy seeding gun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Yeah. Now they had their cattle in the canyon and the sunflowers was all in bloom and everything and they had it drifted so in the canyon they had to go and have a time of getting them cattle out. They had to dig some of these drifts so the cows could get out to get home to get something to eat.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Oh I can't, that's hard to believe to have that happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was right through the end of March it was like that?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: The year she was born. Yeah old Wade Keeney lived on American Ridge and he come to town and he said I'll bet there's 5 ,000 songbirds dead along the road that froze to death from up there coming into town. Well it got down to over 20 some below zero all in just once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it snow too or what happened?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Mr. Riker pleasanted the snow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roy what was your day of birth?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: The Moscow Hotel was the center of everything then and of course then there was Judge Hodgins in the Hodgins Drugstore. Hodgins had been one of the early settlers, very early settlers in the 70s as a boy. He grew up over by Genesee, was the first probate judge of the county when, when, uh, uh, Laetau County was formed. Uh, by an act of Congress and by the way as you know this is about, I guess the only county in the United States was created by an act of Congress. Uh, the first national bank was there on the corner. It was a three story building and up above was a lodge hall. Of course that's been torn down. Across the street, uh, as I remember it there was a barn over there. Back of the, uh, back of the first national bank was the, uh, Carl Grice had his undertaking establishment. Uh, as I remember it the first trust and savings bank was up the street a little ways but after I started to practice law they built the fine building that the, uh, first security bank is now lodged in now. The other bank across where the abstract office and where there's a women's shop there in that building was the Moscow State rank. It was operated by Robert Whittier as president and Harry Whittier as cashier. They were very fine and active people here, uh, businessmen here that, uh, went down in the depression in 1933. Of course, Chris Hagen and Hagen and Cushing had a big meat market and grocery store next to, uh, Creighton's store. And of course Hagen and Cushing had a packing plant out, uh, east of town out in the area where they now have the baseball park and, uh, field out there, soccer field out there. Uh, Creighton's was operating, uh, Vic Ramstet was a younger man then, now gone. His son now operates it, the business.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: And going a little further up the street was the pastime pool hall.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Now the pastime pool hall was the poor man's club. It was a fairly large establishment on one floor, the ground floor. They had a lunch counter, a big one. They had, uh, people go in there and play cards. Uh, they could buy soft drinks and coffee there. But the pastime pool hall stayed open and never closed. It was open day and night. There was a big stove in there.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Anybody could go in there.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: In the wintertime, anybody that was cold could go in there. I remember there was one character called Pozy who lived in a shack down on the north end of town here. He used to go in there. He was kind of a half -witted fellow. He'd come in there and Art Ransom ran the, uh, Art Ransom ran the, and owned the pastime pool hall. And he lived up in a nice bungalow house up across east, or west of the old courthouse. Ransom was a very interesting man. A very quiet, very, uh, uh, he never got excited. He was always in the background. But as a prosecuting attorney, I can say that there was never a better run at establishing than we had there. And in all the years I was prosecuting attorney, we never had any offenses committed there, never had any trouble with, with liquor. He kept it in perfect order, never had any trouble with gambling.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Uh, hunters used to go in there that would go hunting and get up at three o 'clock in the morning. You could always go in and get a good meal there if you came in the middle of the night.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: And even college students, of course no girl or woman would ever go in there, because that was simply the domain, strictly the domain of the men. But they used to send somebody in to get hamburgers and so on. And really it was an asset to the town. He didn't sell any magazines. He, I can say that it was the poor man's friend. And the lumberjack that came in, I can remember it was a standing joke that when the first snow fell, and a lumberjack would walk out in front, and the snow would be coming down, and somebody would say to him, I'm sorry that you didn't hang on to your summer wages. But they could always go into the pastime. There were some benches there, and of course he did a good business. There was pool tables there. It was frowned on by the very churchy part of town, but actually it was the best run, establishment of sort, that I've ever known. And of course it passed when Art Ransom passed, because somehow he could get along with everybody and he operated it there. Then, of course, further on down and across the street was what had been the old department store, the great big, what's now the Thetuna Apartments. That had gone out, and there were stores in there, and then of course they remodeled it in my early days here in Moscow, that is, when I was practicing law into the Thetuna Apartments. But that had been a great department store in the early days that attracted a lot of business to Moscow. And of course further on down the street, on the east side of Main Street, was the Washburn Wilson Seed Company office, and then of course over toward the railroad where there were warehouses and so on. And of course, to go back on the east side of Main Street, and I'm passing out a lot of, up a lot of places, I know, well of course I should mention that there was the closed harvester plant down there where the early football games had been played. And on the east side of the street, of course, besides what became the Thetuna Apartments, was the Fred Sam's Furniture Store, the Elks Lodge, the old Idaho Hotel, and then later they built the Moscow Grange building there in that area. And of course coming up further uptown was the Willis Drug Store, there was the Moscow State Bank, the abstract office, there was Robbins Pool Hall in there that was run by one of the Robbins brothers, there was another, Grant Robbins, who was a chief of police and a fine officer. Bill Robbins ran the pool hall, and then there was Charlie Bowles, the corner drug store. Charlie was a well -known pioneer druggist. Across the street, of course, was the David's Department Store. Frank David was still alive with his four sons, Homer, Howard, Earl, and Don, but Don had graduated in 1917 and gone back to Harvard. Where later he became dean of the Harvard Business School. I should mention that on Third Street, further on down, was the old Pleasant Home boarding house, the Gryce Undertaking Parlour. There was a, was a, the old business college was up above, oh for a minute I can't think of his name, was up above where the jewelry store and sheep shop is now. Oh, I'll think of his name later. Across the street was Jerry's Cigar Store, where he sold peanuts and hot popcorn, and Jerry had started with a peanut and popcorn stand there on Third Street, and finally it was a portable one that used to be rolled up on wheels, and then he got his little Jerry's store there. And across the street was the, was the old grocery store, I can't remember the name of the grocery store, opposite it was the table supply, a local grocery store. Herman Wilson had operated the store on the side where Jerry's on the north side, but he got into the Washburn Wilson Seed Company, and a fellow by the name of Davis took it over. And this Davis was a very unusual fellow. He had his boys there, and Davis did fairly well with the store, and then he went to Florida. And he proved to be a, a, a very unusual executive. He built up what is now the, the Wynn -Dixie Stores Incorporated, which covers all of Texas. It's the leading mercantile grocery and, and, and food supply chain in Florida. His sons have become vice presidents, and one of them, a graduate of, of the University of Idaho that used to clerk in the store part time, is now one of the vice presidents. A very fine young fellow at Colfax that I knew his family well, from over there, his name is Hollingsworth, Howard Hollingsworth. He's become a real executive now, and a vice president of the Dixie Wynn Stores, and he started his working part time in Davis' store. Now, further on up, of course, Ken Worthy had his theater there, his first theater next to that, and back of that was the, Frost had a, had a garage. And later, that's, was, well it was burned down, and then George Bluer had a garage there. And of course, then there was the post office, and across the street from the post office, I've kind of forgotten what some of the, there didn't seem to be much of a house or two there, and then there was the Egan Hall, there where the, the Market Time Drug Store is. That was the main meeting place for dances, and, and general community meetings were there. And then further up was the old DAR Hall, but the DAR Hall had burned down, not the DAR, I mean the GAR, the Grand Army of the Republic Hall, had burned down before I came to Moscow, but that was pointed out where it was. And just at the corner there, across from where the parking lot by the Methodist Church is, was the, George Lamphere lived there. Now, George Lamphere was a very interesting man. George Lamphere had run the weekly Idaho Post, and then he consolidated with the, with the Star Mirror, and had the Star Mirror. And then later there developed this big fight between Frank B. Robinson's newspaper and the Star Mirror that resulted after Lamphere's death in the merger of the two in what is now the Idahoian, but that was the merger of the News Review and the Moscow Star Mirror. But that's a long story about that. I was involved in that when Dr. Frank B. Robinson had his Psycheana, which was, deserves a story all its own. Now, the lawyers, when I started to practice, oh well I should mention that down the street Charlie Carter had just started his, he had the Carter's Drug Store, he started that in about the same time that I started to practice law. And next to him was the Parisian, which is operated by, uh, Stewart. We called him Fanny Stewart. Gee, Stewart had some wonderful women's clothing there. He used to go back to New York each year and get it. Then the City Hall was next to that. Then across the street was the, uh, R .B. Ward's Hardware and Paint Store. And further on down there were other buildings, Penny's. There was a, there was a service station down in the next block. I've just tried to hit some of the leading businessmen we had here at the time. I ought to mention Hawk & Melgard and Bill Cahill at the First Trust and Savings Bank. Claude Renfrew and John Hecathorn at the First National Bank of Moscow, which was then an independent bank.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Chris Hagen, of course, was an early Senator from this county. Fred Veatch was a prominent real estate and businessman whose business was eventually taken over by Martin Micky. There was George Richardson, who later became mayor of Moscow, who was the station agent for the Spokane & Inland Railway when it came in in 1908 and then later was taken over by the Great Northern Railway. There was Oscar Bonnet, who was with the, who came out from Kansas and was with the Washburn Wilson Seed Company, who later became a wonderfully fine man, whose son was Robert Bonnet, who has carried on the fine tradition of his father for public service. A wonderful family, Oscar Bonnet and his son Robert Bonnet. There was Tim Sullivan, who ran the foreign garage and was one of the directors of the Moscow State Bank. He was the early Ford dealer here and a leading citizen. I've spoken about Joe Gilmore, who operated the Vinegar Works, and of course there was Gub Mix, who became lieutenant governor, who was active in politics, who was a prominent graduate of the university. And there was his brother Frank Mix, who lived out north of town, who took over the old Frank Barton house, that later after Mix died was taken over by the man who ran the creamery here.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Stop it, will you?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: It was taken over by Carter later, of course, Carter is now past in the, what used to be the old Moscow Creamery is now where the city hall is. Now I want to mention, of course, that Moscow's probably most prominent citizen in the state was Jerry Jerome J. Day, the mining man who had his palatial home here in Moscow, but he also had a home in Wallace, and he was a member of the famous Day family with a wealthy mining man. He was a regent in the university and a graduate of the university.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: I think some of the old timers will remember Charlie Thompson, who used to run the old abstract office. And then there was Fred McGowan, who worked for him, an interesting old bachelor who was bald headed and his head came up to a point. But those were some of the old timers in business when I came in here. Now the lawyers, when I came in I was the youngest lawyer, there was all old time lawyers when I came in. I got a chance to go into the office of C .J. Orland, who was an old time lawyer here. His partner, William E. Lee, who had been a prominent lawyer, had just been elected to the Supreme Court of the state of Idaho. And Lee had left his office vacant there, and at the suggestion of Bill Lee, whom I knew, I went in and saw Mr. Orland, told him I wanted to practice law, and he said, well, I'll tell you. Abe, I'll let you come in here. You can have Lee's old office. I'll let you use my library, and you can pay me ten dollars a month rent. And that's how it started out. Then later, when Orland, as a compromise, with several aspirants, was appointed prosecuting attorney on the death of the man who had been elected in prosecuting attorney, Orland was appointed, and he'd take the job only in case he had a deputy to do the run around, and I was appointed the deputy. And then I served for nearly two years as his deputy, and then ran for prosecuting attorney and was elected for four terms myself. Now, the attorneys, the leading attorneys here in Moscow at that time were August H. Oversmith, who was an old time attorney and had practiced at Troy before he came up here, Frank Moore, and his son Latham Moore, who had graduated just before I did, and Latham had started in with his father. Of course, my CJ Orland, with whom I later took me in as a partner, there was George Pickett, who had his office up above the Sam's Furniture Store down toward the Elks Club. There was the man known as Judge Warren Truitt. He was very much older. It was an honorary title of judge for Warren Truitt, a very well thought of handsome old gentleman. Then there was also Judge J. H. Forney, who had been a regent of the university and had been its attorney in the early days. His also was an honorary title of judge because they often would call some venerable old lawyer, well respected, call him judge. Then there was Al Morgan and Bill Morgan, although William Morgan had just gone to Boise, and he later was elected to the Supreme Court, but his brother Al Morgan was practicing here. There was another old attorney in the back end of the First National Building named H. R. Smith. H. R. Smith was a respected old timer here who didn't have any trial practice but had some probate practice. H. R. Smith, I'll always remember, a pleasant old gentleman with a goatee, a staunch congregation church member, the father of Harvey Smith. H. R. Smith had been admitted to the bar back before they had the rule that you had to be admitted to the Supreme Court, and he admitted in territorial days by the district court here. He'd only been admitted to the district court, but he was practicing. Then there was Louis Peterson had been admitted to the bar, but he then was city clerk. There was also Thomas Feeney who was practicing here, F. E. E. N. E. and Adrian Nelson had been admitted to the bar. He was an early graduate of the university, but Adrian Nelson had been elected probate judge and was probate judge, though he had been practicing here. Then there was A. L. Morgenridge who had been a deputy sheriff, I think was the probate judge when I first came to Moscow. Now, we, as I said, there were lots of more trial work then, not only prohibition cases but other cases, very much more trial work. There seemed to be more criminal trial work. Some of the early cases I remember when I first was admitted to the bar was the trial of A. S. Frost for burning down his garage, a trial for arson. That attracted great attention. Frost was charged with burning down his garage for the insurance, he'd heavily insured it. I prosecuted that case in my first years prosecuting attorney and it was a long case. It took us five or six days to trial. We had a jury. The principal witness against Frost was a fellow that the sheriff's force had run down that said he had been hired by Frost to set the building on fire. And it was a bitterly tried case. The case, the evidence was overwhelming against Frost, although Frost, like a lot of other defendants, was able to bring a lot of witnesses as to his good reputation. But this witness testified possibly he was of tranche, that he had been hired by Frost to set the building on fire and told how he did it. But in the instruction to the jury, Judge Steele was the judge then, and it's a rule of law that you may not convict a person of any crime on the uncorroborated testimony of an accomplice. Uncorroborated means that there must be some independent circumstances, apart from the testimony of the accomplice, that would tend to connect the defendant with the offense. Well, old Judge Steele, I don't want to do him too much of an injustice, but in the instruction to the jury he laid down that in great particularity about the corroboration that was necessary for the testimony of an accomplice.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: And he laid it and laid stress in it by tone of voice and everything else.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: I've always suspected he was, well I hate to say this, but a little tied in with both Over Smith and they were great friends, and with Frost, but it was as much the inflection as the long and detailed instruction to the jury anyway. After a prolonged all night session the jury brought in a verdict of acquittal. Now, some of the sheriff's force and I did talk to the jurors afterwards, and without exception they said, well of course we knew he was guilty, but with the instruction the court gave us why we couldn't believe any of his testimony, why we couldn't convict him without that. So Frost was acquitted, but that was a major case and a great disappointment to me, but I had better fortune than a good many others. But that attracted a lot of interest here and in the northwest, this trial of Frost for arson. And as a matter of fact, Frost never did collect his money for the insurance because they refused to pay and finally Frost abandoned an effort to try to force him to pay, so he got nothing out of it and Frost left here afterwards. Now another case, it was about the middle term of when I was prosecuting attorney, was the case of State vs. Orr for the attempted robbery of Tommy Matthews.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Now, T .D.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Matthews was a graduate of the university who had been a track man and was the track coach at the university, I think shortly after I graduated. A very well respected son of pioneer families, the farm was out here north of Moscow. Now after he left his coaching at the university, Matthews operated a lunch counter and coffee shop and soft drink place near the entrance to the university. And across, caddy-corner across from where the LDS building is now, well or was where the LDS building is now, it's near the entrance to the university, I guess that's been torn down now. But it's right almost directly in front of where the front steps of the university is now, and it was a little east of where the Dean J .G. Eldredge's house was. Well now this was a favorite rendezvous of students, and Matthews used to keep it open until midnight at night, it would open in the early morning, they'd get coffee, students would stop and get lunches there. Matthews was a very popular student rendezvous, and he usually closed at midnight and go on home. Matthews lived across town, over on the clear across town and over in the area, the general area where the Roman Catholic Church stands, it's over in there. And it was his custom each night when he closed up to take his proceeds and get into his car and drive on home at night. Well, this particular night, Matthews closed up his place, took his purse with his cash in it and so on, his proceeds for the day, went out and got into his car, drove across after midnight across town. And when he had got across Main Street, and had gotten up in the neighborhood of where the, on First Street, in the neighborhood of where the, well it's the George K. Moody place, where it used to be the parsonage of the Lutheran Church, and up in that corner up here. Somebody rose up from behind and hit him on the head with a, with a, with a, one of these lead, not lead, but shot -loaded, leather -covered, with like blackjack, hit him on the head. And knocked him out, and of course the car ran into the curb, and when he came to, the car was wrecked and his purse was gone.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Well, we won't go into, there's a long story about how we ran the thing down, but of course here was a very popular campus, Karen Business, well -known Moscow character, had been robbed, been knocked in the head with a blackjack when he was going home with the proceeds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you say very, could you say briefly how it was run down?

speaker: Abe Goff
words: I'm curious. Of course, there's a young fellow by the name of Orr, Harry Orr, who's not related to any of the Orrs here now, that for several reasons was suspected he'd been hanging around up there, he'd gone up there and he'd, he'd had gotten into some minor troubles, he'd, and the Sheriff's Office suspected him and others, he'd been up around this place. And there were some other reasons that caused it, connected him with it, but the principal investigator was our own George K. Moody, old Hap Moody, and somehow old Hap, who had always kind of hung around the campus, even those days, could have been interested up there. And he did a lot of questioning and running the thing down, and so old Hap Moody finally, and of course I did a lot of questioning of people for all the incidents of it, and of course talked many times to Matthews, and Matthews did look up and get a hasty look at the man who bent over him and hit him, because the fellow coughed and Matthews turned around and hit him. And the fellow who was hit on the head, he got a glimpse of the fellow, and there was something about him that kind of caused him to, the general idea of the, some of the possible description of about the same size, though it was dark, of this Harry Orr, but there was some light from the street light there, but he couldn't identify him, but it was some kind of a hasty, it could have been him. Well anyway, old Hap Moody went down to the Orr house, finally and shortly after this took place, when he found his suspicions, and he went out in the trash heap, and the, where the ashes were thrown from the Orr house. And he quietly gathered up those ashes, all the ashes he could find, and then he sifted them carefully.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: And, lo and behold, he sifted out the metal part of a purse, a cash, one of these long leather bank purses that you carry cash and bills in. Then he took that out carefully, and shined it up, took it to Matthews, and Matthews, yes, he could identify it, that was his, his, the, that was his purse.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: That was the part, yes, it was a special purse, and it was of a special character, and it came from, I believe, the first trust bank, but there were some special markings on it that he could identify it. Well then there was a lot of other little leads that were worked out, and there was a lot of people in the bank, and there was a lot of interest in this thing. It finally resulted in me filing a criminal charge against, against Orr for the attempted robbery of, and criminal assault on T .D. Matthews.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: We tried the case, and it was a well tried case, Orr employed A .L.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Morgan, and A .L. Morgan was an able and excellent lawyer. He later became a very good district judge here, and he was an able lawyer, and I respected him, and he defended Orr ably, and, but finally the jury, principally upon the testimony of Hap Moody, convicted Harry Orr. Morgan appealed it, or Orr appealed it to the Supreme Court, and that was a hard fought case in the Supreme Court. The Attorney General, the Assistant Attorney General took a rather dim view of the prosecution, but I secured the permission of the Supreme Court to argue the case, that it was on technical grounds, on some of the technical points of the trial that the appeal was taken. And the Deputy Attorney General argued it on the ground, that on technical grounds it ought to be set aside on some of the proceedings in the trial of it. I think we tried it by old Judge Steele, and George Steele by that time was not in his prime, and there were some questions, but I secured the permission, went down, secured the permission of the Supreme Court to argue it. I argued it on behalf of the state, and I argued it before the Supreme Court myself. I made part of the argument. And fortunately, the Supreme Court said the evidence was overwhelming. They disregarded some of this other, and Orr was convicted, and of course was sent to the penitentiary. But it was certainly a case that attracted widespread interest here, and throughout the whole Northwest. We had some reporters here from quite a few places. It was a very interesting case. Now of course, there are a lot of other cases. I tried lots of other cases, and later went out as I was re -elected in 1932 when the big landslide and democratic landslide took place, and then I didn't run again. And I had been pressured to run for district judge, but I came out. I had to have my fill of public office at the time, and I built up a very substantial practice, so I came out strongly for A. L. Morgan to be the district judge, and he eventually was elected.

speaker: Abe Goff
words: Maybe I'm wrong about, maybe Gillis Hodge was the judge when the Orr case was tried, but Gillis Hodge had been appointed after Steele's death. And then he had been elected district judge, Gillis Hodge Hyatt, and then Morgan was elected after Hodge died and proved a very good district judge here. Then I was elected to the State Bar Commission, and then as president of the State Bar, and then I was elected to the State Senate, and then World War II came along, and I was called to active duty as a reserve officer in the summer of 1921. Served five years, then came back to be elected to Congress, then served two years, was defeated in the democratic landslide of Truman. I had won out in what had been a democratic district for 13 terms by a 2000 vote. I was defeated by the same thing two years later. Then the next time I decided I'd either get in or out of politics, and I ran for the Senate in the Republican primary, didn't make it, and I was out of politics. Thought I was, went back to my law practice, was enjoying it until I was induced to take the post as general counsel of the post office department, appointed by President Eisenhower, served as general counsel, that's the chief legal officer of the U .S. post office department, naturally with a big staff of lawyers, in the biggest, the largest government department except for defense, but of course the Army, Navy, and Air Force, but you see there's 600 ,000 employees in the post office department. They have an employee in every crossroads in America. That was the biggest civilian legal job in government. From that I was, after four years, appointed as, that is outside of the attorney general's office, I'd say that it's the most important department counsel's job. And then from that I was appointed by President Eisenhower to the interstate commerce commission where I served for nine years as a member and as chairman, resigning in 1960, late 1967 after nine years to come back to my home here in the Palouse country, which I'd always loved and where I wanted to spend the rest of my days because I like it out here. I had opportunities to stay in Washington, but as my wife said to me, if you take some of these other opportunities you have here, we'll just never get back to Moscow and our home and to the Palouse country and to the Pacific Northwest. Let's go if we're going to go now. So I came back and I've never regretted it because the people here is where my old time friends are, where the people are industrious, law abiding, God fearing, and patriotic, where there's the mountains near and the lakes, the opportunity for recreation. And thank God there isn't the racial strife nor the traffic that we find around a big city.
